home.title: TreeMapper
home.description: Learn how to map, monitor, and manage tree planting interventions with our comprehensive guides and tutorials.

introduction.title: Introduction to TreeMapper
introduction.description: TreeMapper is a digital monitoring and management platform developed by Plant-for-the-Planet to support high-quality, transparent, and science-based forest restoration.
introduction.intro: TreeMapper is designed to help restoration projects move beyond simple tree-count reporting and towards data-driven ecosystem monitoring. It enables organizations to document planting activities, monitor tree survival and growth, manage species and interventions, and analyze restoration progress over time, all in a standardized and verifiable way.
introduction.platformIntro: The platform consists of two tightly integrated components:
introduction.mobileAppDesc: built for on-ground data collection, even in remote and offline environments
introduction.webDashboardDesc: built for project management, data analysis, and collaboration
introduction.platformSummary: Together, they provide an end-to-end solution for restoration teams, NGOs, researchers, and funders to plan, monitor, verify, and improve restoration outcomes.
introduction.purposeIntro: TreeMapper was created to address common challenges in restoration projects, such as:
introduction.challenge1: Inconsistent data collection across teams and regions
introduction.challenge2: Limited visibility into long-term survival and impact
introduction.challenge3: Difficulty managing species, plots, and interventions at scale
introduction.challenge4: Lack of transparent and auditable monitoring data
introduction.purposeSummary: By combining field-level data capture with centralized analytics and management, TreeMapper supports credible restoration aligned with ecological best practices rather than just planting numbers.
introduction.user4: Donors and partners seeking transparent impact reporting
introduction.whoForSummary: It is especially suited for projects that require long-term monitoring, species-level insights, and clear accountability.
introduction.whatsNewIntro: TreeMapper has recently evolved into a more powerful and scalable platform with:
introduction.newFeature1: A web dashboard offering advanced functionality such as role management, species and intervention management, and analytics
introduction.newFeature2: Enhanced mobile app features, including structured monitoring plots and improved long-term monitoring workflows
introduction.whatsNewSummary: These updates make TreeMapper not just a data collection tool, but a comprehensive restoration management system.

mobileSetup.title: Mobile App Setup
mobileSetup.description: Learn how to install and configure the TreeMapper mobile app on your iOS or Android device.
mobileSetup.reqIOS: iOS 13.0 or later, iPhone 6s or newer
mobileSetup.iosStep1: Open the App Store on your device
mobileSetup.iosStep3: Tap Get or the download icon
mobileSetup.iosStep4: Authenticate with Face ID, Touch ID, or your Apple ID password
mobileSetup.iosStep5: Wait for the download and installation to complete
mobileSetup.androidStep4: Wait for the download and installation to complete
mobileSetup.firstLaunchIntro: When you first open TreeMapper, you'll need to complete these steps (sign-in is optional):
mobileSetup.permissionsIntro: TreeMapper will request the following permissions:
mobileSetup.permLocation: Required for GPS tracking and mapping interventions
mobileSetup.permCamera: Needed to take photos of trees and sites
mobileSetup.permStorage: To save photos and offline map data
mobileSetup.permNotifications: Optional, for sync status and reminders
mobileSetup.permissionWarning: Location permission is required for TreeMapper to function properly. Without it, you won't be able to map tree locations or create interventions.
mobileSetup.signInIntro: TreeMapper can be used completely without signing in. You can collect data, take photos, and export your data directly from the app without any account.
mobileSetup.offlineUseNote: You can use TreeMapper fully offline to collect tree planting data, take measurements, document with photos, and export your data. No account or internet connection required for data collection.
mobileSetup.signInBenefitsIntro: However, signing in unlocks additional features:
mobileSetup.benefitCloudBackup: Upload your data to secure cloud storage for backup and access from multiple devices
mobileSetup.benefitProjectMgmt: Create and manage projects, collaborate with team members, and organize your restoration activities
mobileSetup.benefitWebDashboard: View and analyze your data on the web dashboard with advanced analytics, reporting, and visualization tools
mobileSetup.benefitTeamCollab: Share data with your organization and work together on restoration projects
mobileSetup.signInOptionsIntro: If you choose to sign in, you have two options:
mobileSetup.optionSignIn: If your organization has already created an account for you, use your credentials to sign in
mobileSetup.optionCreateAccount: Register a new account with your email address
mobileSetup.projectSelectionTitle: 3. Select or Create a Project (If Signed In)
mobileSetup.projectSelectionIntro: If you signed in, you'll need to select or create a project to work on. Projects are organizational units that group your tree planting and monitoring activities.
mobileSetup.projectOption1: If you were invited to a project, select it from the list
mobileSetup.projectOption2: If you're starting fresh, create a new project by tapping "Create Project"

mobileSetup.whatsNextIntro: Now that your app is set up, you're ready to start using TreeMapper:

quickStart.title: Quick Start Guide
quickStart.description: Get up and running with TreeMapper in 5 minutes.
quickStart.intro: This guide will get you started with TreeMapper quickly. Follow these steps to begin tracking your tree planting and forest monitoring activities.
quickStart.step1Title: Step 1: Install the Mobile App (2 minutes)
quickStart.step1Item1: Download TreeMapper from the App Store (iOS) or Play Store (Android)
quickStart.step1Item2: Open the app and grant location and camera permissions
quickStart.step1Item3: Sign in or create a new account
quickStart.step2Title: Step 2: Create or Join a Project (1 minute)
quickStart.step2Item1: If invited to a project, select it from the list
quickStart.step2Item2: Otherwise, tap "Create Project" and fill in the details
quickStart.step2Item3: Give your project a name and select your region
quickStart.step3Title: Step 3: Create Your First Intervention (2 minutes)
quickStart.step3Item2: Tap the + button to start a new intervention
quickStart.step3Item3: Choose "Tree Planting" as the intervention type
quickStart.step3Item4: Mark the location on the map
quickStart.step3Item6: Select tree species and enter count
quickStart.interventionsDesc: Interventions are any forestry actions you document—tree planting, removal, maintenance, or monitoring. Each intervention has a location, type, date, and associated data like species and photos.
quickStart.offlineModeDesc: TreeMapper works completely offline. All data is stored on your device and syncs automatically when you reconnect to the internet. This makes it perfect for remote field work.
quickStart.projectsSitesDesc: Projects are top-level organizational units. Sites are specific geographic locations within a project where interventions take place. You can have multiple sites per project.
quickStart.projectsSitesLink: Learn more about projects and sites →
quickStart.taskPlantTreesDesc: Create a tree planting intervention with location and species data
quickStart.taskSetupPlotDesc: Establish a standardized plot for long-term forest research
quickStart.taskMeasurementsDesc: Record tree height, diameter, and other scientific data

interventions.title: Understanding Interventions
interventions.description: Learn about interventions, the core concept in TreeMapper for documenting forestry actions.
interventions.whatIsIntro1Rest: is any documented forestry action or activity in TreeMapper. It represents work performed at a specific location and time, such as planting trees, removing invasive species, maintaining existing forests, or conducting observations.
interventions.whatIsIntro2: Every intervention contains key information including location, date, type, species involved, photos, and measurements—creating a comprehensive record of your forestry work.
interventions.exampleImageDesc: Visual representation of an intervention with all its components
interventions.typesIntro: TreeMapper supports multiple intervention types to cover various forestry activities:
interventions.singleTreeDesc: Planting one tree at a specific point location
interventions.multiTreeDesc: Planting multiple trees within a defined area (polygon)
interventions.regenerationDesc: Documenting natural forest regeneration or assisted regeneration efforts
interventions.maintenanceDesc: General tree care activities like pruning, watering, or mulching
interventions.fireSuppressionDesc: Firebreak creation or fire prevention measures
interventions.fencingDesc: Installing protective barriers around planted areas
interventions.deadTreeRemovalDesc: Removing deceased trees for safety or land management
interventions.observationDesc: Recording observations without physical work (wildlife, disease, etc.)
interventions.remeasurementDesc: Follow-up measurements to track tree growth over time
interventions.requiredFieldsIntro: Every intervention must have these essential components:
interventions.typeDesc: The kind of intervention (planting, maintenance, etc.)
interventions.projectDesc: Which project this intervention belongs to
interventions.photosDesc: Visual documentation of the intervention site
interventions.treeCountDesc: Number of trees planted or affected
interventions.creator: Creator
interventions.syncStatusDesc: Whether data has been uploaded to the server
interventions.workflowStep1Desc: Open the app, navigate to the location, and start a new intervention
interventions.workflowStep2Desc: Add photos, select species, enter measurements, and record details
interventions.workflowStep3Desc: Intervention is saved on your device, even without internet
interventions.workflowStep4Desc: When online, data automatically syncs to the server
interventions.workflowStep5Desc: View intervention data on the web dashboard for reporting and analytics
interventions.pointDesc: Point interventions are marked with a single GPS coordinate representing the exact location of a tree or feature.
interventions.polygonDesc: Polygon interventions define a boundary by walking the perimeter and recording GPS points. TreeMapper automatically calculates the area and displays it on maps.
interventions.pointVsPolygonImageDesc: Visual comparison showing point marker and polygon boundary on map
interventions.incompleteDesc: Interventions saved as drafts without all required information. You can return to complete them later.
interventions.pendingSyncDesc: Complete interventions stored locally but not yet uploaded to the server. They'll sync automatically when internet is available.
interventions.syncedDesc: Interventions successfully uploaded to the server. Data is backed up and visible in the web dashboard.
interventions.syncFailedDesc: Interventions that encountered errors during sync. Usually due to server issues or connectivity problems. You can retry syncing from the Interventions tab.
interventions.accuracyTip1: Wait for strong GPS signal (accuracy < 10 meters) before marking locations
interventions.accuracyTip2: Stand at the tree or center of planting area for point interventions
interventions.accuracyTip3: Walk the actual perimeter slowly for polygon interventions
interventions.documentationTip1: Take photos from multiple angles (overview, close-up, context)
interventions.documentationTip2: Include people or objects for scale reference
interventions.documentationTip4: Add descriptive notes about site conditions
interventions.organizationTip1: Use consistent naming conventions for sites
interventions.organizationTip2: Tag interventions with relevant labels for easy filtering
interventions.organizationTip3: Record interventions promptly while details are fresh
interventions.organizationTip4: Sync data regularly to prevent loss
interventions.question1Title: Can I edit an intervention after syncing?
interventions.question1Answer: Yes, but with some limitations. You can edit most fields like notes, species, and measurements. However, changing the location or intervention type may require administrator approval depending on your project settings.
interventions.question2Title: What happens to interventions if I delete the app?
interventions.question2Answer: Synced interventions are safe on the server. Unsynced interventions will be lost unless you sync before deleting. Always sync your data before uninstalling the app or switching devices.
interventions.question3Title: How many interventions can I create offline?
interventions.question3Answer: There's no practical limit. You can create hundreds of interventions offline, limited only by your device storage (primarily photos). Each intervention is small (~1KB of data without photos).

plots.title: Plots & Plot Groups
plots.description: Understand monitoring plots and plot groups, key concepts for systematic long-term forest monitoring in TreeMapper.
plots.whatAreIntro1Rest: is a specific, clearly defined area within a restoration site that is observed repeatedly over time. Monitoring plots provide a structured framework for collecting consistent, comparable data about forest restoration progress.
plots.whatAreIntro2: By repeatedly collecting data from the same defined area, you can track changes in vegetation growth, species composition, and ecosystem recovery over time. This systematic approach enables evidence-based assessment of restoration effectiveness.
plots.conceptImageDesc: Visual representation of a monitoring plot as a defined area for repeated observation
plots.measuringEffectivenessDesc: Quantify how well restoration interventions are working by comparing data over time
plots.scientificRigorDesc: Provide standardized, repeatable data collection methods that meet scientific standards
plots.comparativeAnalysisDesc: Compare restored areas with control areas (areas without interventions) to understand restoration impact
plots.longTermTrackingDesc: Establish baseline data and track changes over months, years, or decades
plots.evidenceBasedDesc: Make informed decisions about restoration strategies based on actual data rather than assumptions
plots.plotTypesIntro: TreeMapper supports two main types of monitoring plots:
plots.interventionPlotsIntro: are areas where restoration activities have taken place, such as:
plots.interventionPlotsDesc: These plots help you measure the direct impact of your restoration work by tracking how planted trees and restored areas develop over time.
plots.controlPlotsIntro: represent areas without active interventions. They serve as reference points for comparison, helping you understand:
plots.controlPlotsItem3: The difference restoration makes compared to untouched areas
plots.controlPlotsDesc: By comparing intervention plots with control plots, you can quantify the actual impact of your restoration efforts.
plots.interventionVsControlImageDesc: Visual comparison showing intervention and control plot concepts
plots.plotComponentsIntro: Each monitoring plot contains several key components:
plots.name: Name
plots.dimensionsDesc: Size and shape of the plot area
plots.plantLevelDataIntro: Within each plot, you can record individual plant data, including:
plots.measurementsDesc: Height, diameter, and other scientific data
plots.plotComponentsImageDesc: Diagram showing plot structure with plants, measurements, and observations
plots.plantTimelineIntro1: One of the most powerful features of monitoring plots is the ability to track individual plants over time through a
plots.plantTimelineItem1: Record multiple measurements as plants grow
plots.plantTimelineItem2: Update measurements if corrections are needed
plots.plantTimelineItem3: Track survival rates by marking plants as deceased
plots.plantTimelineItem4: Create a complete historical record of each plant's development
plots.plantTimelineDesc: This longitudinal data collection supports robust analysis of restoration outcomes and helps identify which species and strategies are most successful.
plots.plotGroupsIntro: allow you to organize multiple monitoring plots together for easier management and analysis.
plots.groupStructureItem2: Each monitoring plot can belong to
plots.groupStructureItem3: Groups help organize plots with shared characteristics or objectives
plots.whenToUseIntro: Plot groups are particularly useful for:
plots.controlVsInterventionDesc: Group all control plots together and all intervention plots together for easy comparison
plots.longTermResearchDesc: Organize plots by research site or study area
plots.specificStrategiesDesc: Group plots that use the same restoration approach or technique
plots.geographicOrgDesc: Group plots by location, watershed, or ecological zone
plots.timeBasedStudiesDesc: Organize plots by planting year or monitoring period
plots.plotGroupsImageDesc: Visual representation of how multiple plots can be organized into groups
plots.benefitsIntro: Using plot groups provides several advantages:
plots.simplifiedMgmtDesc: Easier to navigate and manage large numbers of plots
plots.bulkAnalysisDesc: Analyze results across related plots simultaneously
plots.organizedReportingDesc: Generate reports for specific groups of plots
plots.clearStructureDesc: Maintain logical organization as your monitoring program grows
plots.plotsVsInterventionsIntro: It's important to understand the difference between
plots.keyDiff1: document specific actions or events (planting, removal, maintenance) at a point in time
plots.keyDiff2: are long-term observation areas where you repeatedly collect data over time
plots.keyDiff3Rest: monitoring plots to document what was done
plots.keyDiff4: Monitoring plots provide the framework for tracking the
plots.establishBoundariesDesc: Use GPS and physical markers to ensure plots can be accurately relocated
plots.consistentMethodologyDesc: Use the same measurement protocols and timing for all plots in a group
plots.regularMonitoringDesc: Schedule regular remeasurement visits to track changes over time
plots.documentEverythingDesc: Record environmental conditions, weather, and any unusual events during monitoring
plots.usePlotGroupsDesc: Organize plots logically from the start to make management easier as your program grows
plots.whatsNextIntro: Now that you understand plots and plot groups, explore these guides:
plots.nextLink1: Set up your first monitoring plot
plots.nextLink2Desc: and how they relate to plots
plots.nextLink4: View plot data on the web dashboard

projectsSites.title: Projects vs Sites
projectsSites.description: Understand the difference between projects and sites in TreeMapper and how they work together to organize your restoration work.
projectsSites.whatIsProjectIntro2: is the core container for restoration in TreeMapper. It represents a restoration initiative or program where all related activities are grouped together—interventions, sites, monitoring plots, team members, and analytics.
projectsSites.whatIsProjectIntro3: When you sign in to the mobile app or start using the web dashboard, you will be asked to create or select a project so that all your restoration efforts are linked to the right context. You can create as many projects as you need.
projectsSites.projectOverviewImageDesc: Screenshot showing project overview in the web dashboard
projectsSites.projectDetailsIntro: When creating a project, you typically provide:
projectsSites.name: Name
projectsSites.nameDesc: The name of the restoration project
projectsSites.locationDesc: Optional geographic location or region for the project, selected on the map
projectsSites.projectDetailsDesc1: Once saved, the project is created and you can start using other features such as team management, site creation, interventions, monitoring plots, and analytics. Project details can always be edited later from the project edit section.
projectsSites.projectDetailsDesc2: Projects can be created from both the
projectsSites.whatIsSiteIntro2: is a specific polygon area within a project where tree planting and other restoration activities take place. While a project defines the overall initiative, sites define the actual geographic areas on the map.
projectsSites.whatIsSiteIntro4: This allows you to represent multiple planting areas, farms, reserves, or landscape units under the same project.
projectsSites.sitesOnMapImageDesc: Screenshot showing multiple sites within a single project
projectsSites.siteDetailsIntro: When creating a site, you typically provide:
projectsSites.boundaryDesc: A polygon defining the site area
projectsSites.definingBoundariesIntro: You can define site boundaries in multiple ways:
projectsSites.drawPolygonDesc: Manually draw the boundary over the map to mark the site
projectsSites.uploadGeoJSONDesc: Upload an existing GIS file if you already have an accurate site boundary
projectsSites.traceWithGPSDesc: In the mobile app, walk along the border of the site and trace the boundary using GPS when no accurate GeoJSON is available
projectsSites.siteBoundaryMethodsImageDesc: Diagram showing drawing, uploading GeoJSON, and tracing with GPS for site boundaries
projectsSites.accessPermissionsIntro: Projects and sites both play a role in access management, but at different levels of granularity.
projectsSites.projectLevelAccessIntro: At the project level, access is managed via
projectsSites.ownerAdminDesc: Full project management, including team, sites, and settings
projectsSites.contributorDesc: Can contribute data to the project (interventions, monitoring, etc.)
projectsSites.observerDesc: Read-only access to project data for analysis and validation
projectsSites.siteLevelAccessIntro: Sites also have access management so you can give limited access to specific sites for certain team members. This is useful when:
projectsSites.siteLevelAccessItem1: Different teams work on different areas within the same project
projectsSites.siteLevelAccessItem2: You want to restrict contributors to only some sites
projectsSites.siteLevelAccessItem3: External partners are involved in only part of the project area
projectsSites.projectSiteAccessImageDesc: Screenshot showing project-level and site-level access settings
projectsSites.howWorkTogetherTitle: How Projects and Sites Work Together
projectsSites.howWorkTogetherItem1Rest: defines the overall restoration initiative (who, why, and high-level where)
projectsSites.howWorkTogetherItem2: define the specific geographic areas (exact where) where restoration happens
projectsSites.howWorkTogetherItem3: Interventions, monitoring plots, and plant data are associated with sites and projects
projectsSites.howWorkTogetherItem4: Analytics and reporting aggregate data across sites within a project
projectsSites.quickComparisonProject: Who is involved, what the initiative is, and overall scope
projectsSites.quickComparisonSite: Where exactly restoration takes place on the map
projectsSites.quickComparisonItem3: One project → many sites; each site belongs to a single project
projectsSites.quickComparisonItem4: Project-level roles manage overall access; site-level access refines who can work where
projectsSites.nextLink1Desc: to start a new restoration initiative
projectsSites.nextLink2Desc: within your project to define restoration areas
projectsSites.nextLink3Desc: for detailed long-term monitoring inside sites

dataExport.overviewIntro1: All data registered using the TreeMapper app can be exported in
dataExport.overviewIntro3: format. This allows you to use your restoration data in external tools for analysis, reporting, or integration with other systems.
dataExport.exportingMobileIntro1: From the mobile app, you can export
dataExport.exportingMobileIntro2: as a GeoJSON file. This exported file can then be imported into any ArcGIS-supported platform or other GIS tools for spatial analysis and visualization.
dataExport.mobileExportImageDesc: Screenshot showing GeoJSON export option for a single intervention in the mobile app
dataExport.howToExportMobileItem1: Open the intervention you want to export
dataExport.howToExportMobileItem2: Tap the export or share option
dataExport.howToExportMobileItem4: Share or save the file to your preferred location
dataExport.exportingWebIntro2: , use the web dashboard. The web dashboard provides more comprehensive export options, including the ability to filter data by time period.
dataExport.webDashboardExportImageDesc: Screenshot showing data export options in the web dashboard
dataExport.timeBasedFilteringIntro2: to export data from a specific time period. This is useful when you need to generate reports for a particular month, quarter, or year.
dataExport.availableFormatsIntro: Currently, the web dashboard allows you to export all your data in Excel format:
dataExport.excelFormatDesc: Export all intervention and restoration data in a spreadsheet format, ideal for analysis in Excel, Google Sheets, or other spreadsheet applications
dataExport.additionalExportsIntro: Beyond intervention data, you can also export other types of data from their respective sections in the web dashboard:
dataExport.speciesDataDesc: Export species-level data from the species management section. This includes all tree species records, counts, and related information collected across your projects.
dataExport.teamDataDesc: Export team member information and activity data from the team management section. This is useful for tracking contributions and generating team performance reports.
dataExport.gisAnalysisDesc: Export GeoJSON from mobile and import into ArcGIS, QGIS, or other mapping platforms for advanced spatial analysis
dataExport.reportingDesc: Export Excel data from the web dashboard to create custom reports for stakeholders
dataExport.dataBackupDesc: Regularly export your data as a backup or for archival purposes
dataExport.integrationDesc: Use exported data to integrate with other data management or reporting systems

remeasurement.title: Remeasurement
remeasurement.description: Learn how to track tree growth over time by remeasuring trees in TreeMapper.
remeasurement.overviewIntro2: to track their growth and health. Both
remeasurement.overviewIntro4: can be remeasured, allowing you to build a comprehensive history of each tree's development.
remeasurement.howToRemeasureIntro: Follow these steps to remeasure a tree from the mobile app:
remeasurement.step1Desc: Open the mobile app and tap on the intervention containing the tree you want to remeasure
remeasurement.step2Desc: Choose the specific tree you want to remeasure from the intervention
remeasurement.step3Desc: Select the 'Measure' option to record new measurements
remeasurement.step4Desc: Enter the measurement details (height, diameter, etc.) and add an updated photo
remeasurement.step5Desc: Save the remeasurement to complete the process
remeasurement.remeasurementFlowImageDesc: Screenshots showing the step-by-step remeasurement process in the mobile app
remeasurement.recordingDeadTreesIntro: If a tree has died, you can record this using the same workflow. Instead of adding new measurements:
remeasurement.recordingDeadTreesItem1: Select the tree from the intervention
remeasurement.recordingDeadTreesItem3: Provide the reason for the tree's death
remeasurement.recordingDeadTreesItem4: Add any additional comments or observations
remeasurement.recordingDeadTreesItem5: Optionally add a photo documenting the condition
remeasurement.recordingDeadTreesDesc: This helps maintain accurate records of tree survival rates and identify potential issues affecting your restoration sites.
remeasurement.measurementHistoryIntro1: All tree remeasurements are stored as a
remeasurement.measurementHistoryIntro2: and attached to that specific tree. You can view the complete measurement history from the
remeasurement.measurementHistoryItem2: Growth metrics over time (height, diameter, etc.)
remeasurement.treeTimelineImageDesc: Screenshot showing the measurement history timeline for a tree
remeasurement.remeasurementRemindersIntro1: TreeMapper can help you stay on schedule with your monitoring activities. From the mobile app, you can enable
